title: Intermediary-based equity term structure
abstract: We demonstrate that a financial intermediary-based asset pricing model offers a compelling explanation for a new set of conditional moments of equity term structure and convenience yields. The model‚Äôs key mechanism is that the time-varying tightness of intermediaries‚Äô leverage constraints drives significant mean reversion in the price of risk. This model guides us in devising a novel empirical methodology to estimate the tightness of these constraints (i.e., the Relative Tightness Index) from cross-sectional returns of various asset classes. Our findings affirm that this measure significantly drives the dynamics of equity yield slope and convenience yields, both empirically and quantitatively.

author.Kai Li*: Peking University HSBC Business School and PHBS Sargent Institute of Quantitative Economics and Finance, Nanshan District, Shenzhen, 518055, China
author.Chenjie Xu: Shanghai University of Finance and Economics, Yangpu District, Shanghai, 200433, China

article.1. Introduction: In this paper, we study the cyclical variation in the equity term structure. New empirical facts show that the term structure of equity yields is highly procyclical ‚Äì that is, it is positively sloped in expansions and negatively sloped in recessions ‚Äì whereas the equity term premium is countercyclical. Through offering an equity yield decomposition framework, we decompose the term structure of equity yields into an equity term premium component and a mean reversion component with respect to the expected changes in future yields. We show that, in order to reconcile the seemingly contradictory negative relation between equity yield curves and the equity term premium, the second mean reversion component must be strong enough to drive the procyclical fluctuations in the overall equity yield curve, although the first component still remains countercyclical. We propose and demonstrate that a financial intermediary-based asset pricing model offers a compelling explanation of the cyclical pattern of the equity term structure. Our model features fast mean reversion of risk prices, driven by time-varying leverage constraints. Guided by the model implied asset pricing conditions, we develop a novel empirical approach to estimate constraint tightness from the cross-sectional returns of various asset classes and show that this measure significantly drives the time-series fluctuation in the equity yield slope in the data. The estimated constraint tightness offers valuable insights into the intermediary stochastic discount factor, serving as a unique bridge between the data and model. Utilizing this key piece of information, we undertake a rigorous calibration of our model, demonstrating that intermediaries are a quantitatively plausible channel to account for equity term structure dynamics. Furthermore, our model can also generate countercyclical convenience yields that predict bond risk premium, as documented in Van Binsbergen et al. (2021).The literature on the equity term structure has primarily focused on its unconditional average slope, using the yield or return spread between long-maturity and short-maturity dividend claims. Nevertheless, there is still no consensus regarding whether the unconditional slope is downward-sloping or upward-sloping (Van Binsbergen et al., 2012; Van Binsbergen et al., 2013; Bansal et al., 2021). Meanwhile, the literature has documented robust empirical evidence about the conditional equity term structure. Van Binsbergen et al. (2013) document that the equity yield curve is procyclical; in particular, it is positively sloped in expansions and negatively sloped in recessions. On the other hand, Gormsen (2021) documents a countercyclical equity term structure of holding-period returns, suggesting that price fluctuations are primarily driven by long-maturity risks, not short-maturity risks.To reconcile these seemingly contradictory findings, we present a decomposition framework for equity yields, similar to the term structure of interest rates literature. Our framework decomposes the equity yield slope into an equity term premium component and a mean reversion component concerning expected changes in equity yields. Despite the countercyclical nature of the equity term premium, the mean reversion component predominantly drives the procyclical fluctuations in the overall term structure of equity yields. During the 2008 financial crisis, the equity yield curve exhibited a steep downward slope. This observation might lead to the conjecture that the equity term premium was significantly negative, causing higher returns for short-maturity dividends. However, the countercyclical nature of the equity term premium suggests that long-maturity claims have relatively high returns in bad times. Our decomposition framework strongly predicts that the downward sloping equity yield curve in bad times is a result of the mean reversion component, meaning investors expect the yields to revert to their means quickly. This insight highlights the significance of the mean reversion component in reconciling the seemingly contradictory negative relation between equity yield curves and the equity term structure.We propose that financial intermediaries are a quantitatively plausible channel to account for the cyclical patterns of the equity term structure. We embed a financial intermediary sector with a leverage constraint into an endowment economy, following Gertler and Kiyotaki (2010). The leverage constraint opens up an endogenous channel of a countercyclical market price for the consumption shock, therefore generating a strong enough mean reversion of the risk premium to reconcile the cyclical pattern of equity term structure. As financial intermediaries face tighter constraints, the marginal value of net worth increases and becomes more sensitive to aggregate consumption growth shocks. This leads to an endogenous increase in the risk price with constraint tightness at an increasing rate, which is the key to generating a strong mean reversion effect for the discount rate. During recessions, when intermediary net worth is low, a slight mean reversion of net worth causes a significant reduction in risk prices and the risk premium. Meanwhile, high expected equity returns during these periods facilitate net worth recovery. In terms of computation, rather than using a log-linear approximation method, we employ a global method that allows for occasionally binding constraints to solve the model. This approach is essential for capturing the nonlinear dynamics in the binding region.In exploring the relationship between financial intermediaries and asset prices, we adopt a model-guided approach to estimate the tightness of intermediary leverage constraints. Using a cross-section of asset returns from He et al. (2017) and proxies for financial intermediary state variables, we construct a relative tightness index (RT index), reflecting the marginal value of intermediary net worth. Intriguingly, this index, despite being estimated from the returns of other asset classes, exhibits superior explanatory power for the dynamics of the equity yield slope compared to other variables. Moreover, it provides key information about the implied intermediary stochastic discount factor, thus enabling us to rigorously calibrate the intermediary model to the data and examine its quantitative performance for asset prices. We demonstrate that our stylized financial intermediary model endogenously generates fruitful implications and performs well in accounting for both unconditional and conditional moments with respect to the equity term structure, including a slightly positive unconditional equity term structure, a procyclical equity yield slope and its sign-switching feature, and a countercyclical equity term premium.In addition to explaining the equity term structure, our model presents insightful implications for the empirical patterns of convenience yields. As a distinct implication from the model, the leverage constraint effectively introduces a wedge between the interest rates of loans with different collateralizability, such as interbank loans and government bonds. The interest rate spread widens significantly during a credit crunch, characterized by a large drop in intermediary net worth. This pattern is consistent with the evidence that high convenience yields coincide with low price‚Äìdividend ratios and tighter intermediary constraints. Additionally, because bond risk premiums and convenience yields share the same risk source in our model, convenience yields can also predict future excess bond returns, consistent with the empirical evidence documented in Van Binsbergen et al. (2021).New facts with respect to the equity term structure have presented a new testing ground for existing asset pricing models, as emphasized by Cochrane (2017). We posit that the cyclical pattern of equity yield slopes imposes a strong discipline on the mean reversion speed in equity yields for any asset pricing models. According to our decomposition framework, this mean reversion in equity yields arises from either the mean reversion of the discount rate or expected dividend growth. By employing various methods to form dividend growth expectations, we demonstrate that approximately 50% of the fluctuations in the equity yield slope can be attributed to shifts in the discount rate slope. A fast mean reversion of risk prices allows intermediary models to achieve a procyclical equity yield slope through the discount rate channel. Nonetheless, in the standard calibration of the long-run risk model (Bansal and Yaron, 2004) and the habit model (Campbell and Cochrane, 1999), the mean reversion speed of discount rates is too slow to account for a procyclical equity yield slope. After incorporating predictable dividend growth into our baseline model, the extended version demonstrates an enhanced ability to account for nearly the entire variation of the equity yield slope.Our paper is directly linked to the literature on the equity term structure. Studies by Van Binsbergen et al., 2012, Van Binsbergen and Koijen, 2017 have documented that the term structure is on average downward-sloping and argue that this is difficult to reconcile with traditional macro-finance models based on dividend strip data facts. Recent research suggests that short duration stocks pay a higher premium than long duration stocks, implying a downward-sloping equity term structure (Weber, 2018; Gon√ßalves, 2021b; Gormsen and Lazarus, 2023). However, other papers have argued that frictions in derivative markets, such as microstructure noise (Boguth et al., 2012), taxes (Schulz, 2016), and dealer funding costs (Song, 2016), can explain the downward-sloping equity term structure. Bansal et al. (2021) argue that the term structure could be upward-sloping when accounting for illiquidity and the lack of representativeness in the short sample. Giglio et al. (2023) estimate a rich affine model using the cross-section of equity returns and find that the model-implied equity term structure is mildly upward-sloping. Gon√ßalves (2021a) document a hump-shaped term structure and use reinvestment risk to rationalize the term structure patterns. The more robust empirical evidence is about the conditional equity term structure ‚Äî that is, a procyclical equity yield slope and a countercyclical equity term premium. To understand this seemingly contradictory evidence, we decompose the equity yield slope into an equity term premium component and a mean reversion component. While Gormsen (2021) focuses on the countercyclical term premium, our paper highlights the dominant role of the procyclical mean reversion component in driving the overall equity yield slope. We further demonstrate that a fast mean reversion speed is essential for a model to fit the data.In addition to the equity term structure literature, our paper is also connected to several strands of literature. First, this paper is related to the theoretical literature on intermediary frictions. The two broad classes of theories are leverage-constraint theories (Geanakoplos and Fostel, 2008; Adrian and Shin, 2010; Brunnemeier and Pedersen, 2009; Danielsson et al., 2011; Geanakoplos, 2012; Adrian and Boyarchenko, 2012; Brunnermeier and Sannikov, 2014; Maggiori, 2017; Ai et al., 2020) and equity risk-capital constraints theories (He and Krishnamurthy, 2013; Brunnemeier and Pedersen, 2009). Both theories begin with the assumption that intermediaries are constrained in raising more equity. They share two common predictions: first, intermediary equity (or net worth) is the key state variable that affects asset prices; second, the effect of intermediary equity on asset prices is nonlinear, with a larger effect when intermediary equity is low. The aim of our paper diverges from the theoretical literature that introduces alternative microfoundations for financial frictions. Instead, we focus on the quantitative asset pricing implication of an intermediary model with a stylized leverage constraint, as described in Gertler and Kiyotaki (2010). Our paper contributes by introducing a novel measure of constraint tightness, thereby bridging the gap between the theoretical model and empirical data. Additionally, we explore the model‚Äôs performance in explaining equity yields and convenience yields.Second, this paper connects to the literature investigating the relationship between intermediary balance sheets and asset prices (Hu et al., 2013; Adrian et al., 2014; He et al., 2017; Haddad and Sraer, 2020; Haddad and Muir, 2021; Du et al., 2023). Closely related to our work, Adrian et al. (2014) and He et al. (2017) demonstrate that shocks to intermediary state variables, such as the leverage of securities broker‚Äìdealers and the equity capital ratio of financial intermediaries, effectively price equity and various other asset classes. Haddad and Muir (2021) show that the financial health of intermediaries has relatively more predictability for asset classes that are more intermediated. Despite these significant advancements in understanding the relationship between intermediary state variables and asset prices, there is an ongoing debate about which specific state variable serves as the optimal indicator of intermediaries‚Äô financial health. Furthermore, the question of whether intermediary leverage is procyclical or countercyclical remains unsolved. Rather than directly using one of these measures, we adopt a model-guided approach and leverage the intermediary state variables from existing literature to estimate the time series of intermediary constraint tightness. We show that the estimated index exhibits a strong relationship with equity yields in dividend futures markets, which are dominated by sophisticated institutional investors.Lastly, this paper is linked to the literature on financial intermediation and interest rate spreads (Bartolini et al., 2011; Krishnamurthy and Vissing-Jorgensen, 2012; Nagel, 2016; Van Binsbergen et al., 2021; Diamond and Van Tassel, 2021; He et al., 2022; Krishnamurthy and Li, 2023). Directly related to our work, Van Binsbergen et al. (2021) measure convenience yield by using the difference between the option-implied risk-free rate and the government bond rate. They find the convenience yield to be countercyclical and able to predict bond excess returns, whereas its term structure slope remains acyclical. Our paper contributes by providing a coherent framework to rationalize these empirical findings.The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 introduces our decomposition framework for equity yield curves and presents evidence on the cyclical pattern of the equity term structure. In Section 3, we describe our financial intermediary asset pricing model and discuss its implications for equity yields and convenience yields. Section 4 estimates the tightness of the intermediary constraint and investigates its connection to asset prices. In Section 5, we use the estimated constraint tightness to guide our calibration and evaluate the quantitative performance of our model. In Section 6, we decompose the variation of equity yield slope into the discount rate channel and expected dividend growth channel, and extend our baseline model with predictable dividend growth. Finally, Section 7 offers concluding remarks. The Internet Appendix provides details on model derivations, data sources, and computational methods.
article.2. An equity yield curve decomposition a: In this section, we introduce a decomposition framework, which is analogous to the term structure of interest rate literature, for instance, Cochrane and Piazzesi (2005), to better understand the determinants of the yield slope. Specifically, we decompose the term structure slope of the (forward) equity yield into two components: an equity term premium component, linked to the term structure slope of the risk premium, and a mean reversion component in relation to the expected change in (forward) equity yields. Our analysis demonstrates that, while the term structure slopes of (forward) equity yields and dividend strip returns share the same information unconditionally, the determinants of these two term structure slopes differ significantly conditionally. Notably, we observe that the equity term premium is countercyclical, whereas expected changes in equity yields are strongly procyclical. Moreover, the expected change component dominates the term premium component, leading to a procyclical equity yield slope.Gormsen (2021) also decomposes the equity yield slope into the equity term premium and expected change components, albeit in a different way.1 His paper emphasizes the countercyclical equity term premium, whereas our study highlights the procyclical expected change in yields. To the best of our knowledge, our paper is the first to employ an accounting identity to decompose the equity yield slope and examine its implications for general equilibrium models. We propose that, in order to fit the empirical dynamics of equity yields, these models should incorporate a strong mean reversion mechanism.2.1. Dividend stripsIn this subsection, we introduce the concept and notations of dividend strips, following the notation of Van Binsbergen et al. (2013). We denote Pn,t as the time t price of a claim on dividend at time t+n. Such a claim is often called the ‚Äúdividend strip‚Äù or ‚Äúzero-coupon equity‚Äù.The one-period log return on the dividend strip, rn,t+1, is defined as the return from buying a maturity-n dividend strip at time t at the price of Pn,t and selling it as a maturity-(n‚àí1) dividend strip at time t+1 at the price of Pn‚àí1,t+1. Specifically, rn,t+1=lnPn‚àí1,t+1Pn,t.We denote the stochastic dividend paid out in n years from today‚Äôs date t as Dt+n. The equity yield at time t with maturity n is then defined as (1)en,t‚â°1nlnDtPn,t=yn,t+Œ∏n,t‚àígn,td.Eq. An index dividend future is an agreement in which, at maturity, the buyer pays the futures price Fn,t, determined today, and in exchange, the seller pays the dollar amount of dividends Dt+n. By no arbitrage, the futures price is Fn,t=Pn,texp(nyn,t). The forward equity yield, denoted as en,tf, can be expressed in terms of the dividend futures price: (2)en,tf‚â°1nlnDtFn,t=Œ∏n,t‚àígn,td.The literature on the equity term structure has debated whether it is unconditionally downward-sloping (Van Binsbergen et al., 2013; Van Binsbergen and Koijen, 2017) or upward-sloping (Bansal et al., 2021). However, regardless of this debate, clear cyclical patterns have been documented in the term structure slopes. Specifically, Van Binsbergen et al. (2013) and Bansal et al. (2021) find that the term structure slope of equity yields, en,t, is procyclical, whereas Gormsen (2021) shows that the term structure slope of dividend strip returns, rn,t+1, is countercyclical.To reconcile this seemingly contradictory empirical evidence, we provide a decomposition method in the following sections to clarify the relationship between the term structure slope of equity yields and dividend strip returns. Additionally, we discuss the economic forces that drive the unconditional and conditional term structure slopes.2.2. (4) highlights the fact that the term premium from equity and bonds determines the slope of dividend strip returns. In Internet Appendix A.1, we provide a detailed derivation of the corresponding terms for the equity term premium in the fundamental pricing equation. We also examine the implications of leading asset pricing models for the equity term premium.Since dividend strip returns are one-period returns, their term structure slope is determined solely by the difference in their riskiness. In contrast, equity yields represent expected returns over different horizons, meaning their term structure slope reflects not only differences in riskiness but also expected changes in riskiness over time. To illustrate this point, we can draw parallels to bond yields Cochrane and Piazzesi (2005) and represent long-maturity equity yields as a combination of expected short-term equity yields and a term premium. In the following analysis, we show that the expected changes component does not affect yield slopes unconditionally, but is key in resolving the discrepancy between the cyclical patterns of strip return slopes and equity yield slopes.2.2.1. Unconditional term structureEq. (7) provides insight into the unconditional expectation of the equity yield slope. By taking an expectation of the equation, we obtain the following expression: (9)E(en,t‚àíe1,t)=1n‚àëi=1nEŒæi,t‚àíŒæ1,t+Œ∂i,t‚àíŒ∂1,t.Comparing this equation with the unconditional term structure slope of the dividend strip return given by Eq. (4), we find that they share the same information. In fact, the unconditional slope of the equity yield is a weighted average of the dividend strip return slope. Since equity yields are typically less affected by liquidity issues than holding period returns (Bansal et al., 2021), they can serve as a better target for examining the unconditional property of the term structure slope of the risk premium.2.2.2. Conditional term structureThe literature documents a seemingly contradictory pattern: a countercyclical equity term premium and a procyclical equity yield curve. However, this discrepancy can be resolved through the decomposition relation outlined in Proposition 2. Specifically, this proposition shows that the conditional term structure slope equals the conditional equity term premium plus the expected changes in equity yields going forward. If the expected changes component is strongly procyclical and dominates the effect from the variation in the equity term premium, a countercyclical equity term premium and a procyclical equity yield slope can coexist.The cyclical pattern of the equity term premium and equity yield slope imposes a strong discipline on the mean reversion speed of equity yields. In recessions, the short-maturity equity yield exceeds the long-term yield, not because short-maturity dividend strips earn higher risk premiums than long-maturity strips, but because equity yields are expected to decrease at a rapid pace that surpasses the upward-sloping tendency induced by the term premium.2.3. If Œ∏ÀÜ1>1, the effect from expected changes dominates the equity term premium, leading to a negative relation between the equity yield slope and the term premium. If Œ∏ÀÜ1<1, the equity yield slope and term premium are positively related. If 0<Œ∏ÀÜ1<1, it indicates that the equity term premium and expected changes work in the same direction, whereas if Œ∏ÀÜ1<0, then the equity term premium and expected changes work in the opposite direction, and the effect from the term premium dominates.Our results are based on S&P 500, Euro Stoxx 50, and Nikkei 225 dividend futures contracts. The data cover the period from December 2004 to February 2017 and are provided by a major financial institution that is active in dividend strips markets.2 We use the mid-prices of the dividend strip contracts on the last trading day of the month to calculate equity yields and returns.Table 1 shows the average equity yield slope and regression results in the data. We report the sample averages of the equity yield difference between 5-year and 1-year dividend futures as well as the monthly regression coefficients of Eqs. (10)‚Äì(13). The standard errors are Newey and West (1987) corrected for 12 lags.We make several observations from the table: First, the signs of the unconditional equity yield slope vary across countries and are insignificant from zero, consistent with the literature about the debate over the sign of the unconditional slope. Second, the equity yield slope is robustly procyclical and predominantly driven by the mean reversion component.The first column shows the average 5-year minus 1-year equity yield slope across different countries. It is positive for the U.S. and Europe but negative for Japan. However, the large standard errors for these estimates make them statistically insignificant from zero.The cyclicality patterns of the equity yield slope and equity term premium are remarkably consistent across countries. First, all the regression coefficients for Eq. (10) are negative, suggesting a procyclical equity yield slope. Second, estimates from Eq. (11) show that ex-ante dividend-price ratios positively predict future return differences between long- and short-maturity dividend strips, indicating a countercyclical equity term premium. Third, the significantly negative estimates of Eq. (12) imply that expected changes in yields are procyclical. Lastly, and most importantly, the expectation hypothesis regression (13) suggests that equity yields move by less than what the expectation hypothesis proposes. Specifically, the point estimate for the U.S. is close to one, indicating that the fluctuation in the equity yield slope is primarily driven by expected changes in equity yields. The regression coefficients of the other two countries are both well above one.In summary, this empirical evidence presents a coherent narrative that the equity term premium and expected changes in yields move in opposite directions, with the effect from expected changes being considerably greater and dominating equity term premium fluctuations. During recessions, long-maturity dividend strips become riskier, causing the equity yield curve to be positively sloped. However, investors also anticipate that high equity yields will revert to their means, leading to downward-sloping yield curves. article.3. An intermediary-based asset pricing m: 3.1. MotivationIntermediary asset pricing models provide a potential explanation for the observed dynamics of the term structure of the equity yield. Several key aspects of these models align with the observed behavior, which further motivates our study of the equity term structure implications of a financial intermediary model.First, a critical feature of intermediary asset pricing models is the presence of a strongly mean-reverting price of risk, particularly during adverse economic conditions. These models exhibit high nonlinearities in the price of risk (e.g., the risk premium ‚Äúspikes‚Äù when constraints become binding) and strong mean reversion in such states. This mean reversion arises because high risk premia combined with leverage tend to quickly increase net worth, resulting in a lower price of risk in future states.Second, the dividend future markets examined in this study are likely dominated by sophisticated financial institutions, which are precisely the entities for which intermediary asset pricing theories are expected to apply. This is consistent with the models‚Äô assumptions, as the typical household is unlikely to engage in trading dividend futures.Third, we observe that the model-based estimate of intermediary constraint tightness is closely related to the equity yield slope. In Section 4, we use a model-guided approach to construct a relative tightness index (RT index) that measures the marginal value of banks‚Äô net worth. Fig. 1 displays both the realized term structure slope of forward equity yields and a fitted curve from a simple univariate linear regression with the RT index as the explanatory variable. It is noteworthy that we did not use the information about the term structure of the equity yield when measuring the RT index. Nevertheless, the fitted curve closely aligns with the observed term structure dynamics, further supporting the applicability of financial intermediary asset pricing models in explaining the term structure of the equity yield. We also plot the estimated equity yield slope from Giglio et al. (2023) as an out-of-sample test. Although the estimation method and data source of the two fitted series differ significantly, the results in terms of equity term structures are similar in both level and dynamics.Motivated by these observations, we first present our model setup in Section 3.2. Following that, Section 3.3 detail the pricing mechanisms of the model. Building on this, we further explore the implications of our model on the term structure of equity yields and convenience yields in Sections 3.3.2 Equity term structure, 3.3.3 The convenience yield.3.2. Model setupWe incorporate a financial intermediary sector with a leverage constraint, as described by Gertler and Kiyotaki (2010), into a standard Lucas (1978) endowment economy. Equity term structureBesides the stylized patterns on market return, our model is a potential candidate to explain the stylized fact that (1) the equity term premium is countercyclical, and (2) the term structure slope of the equity yield is procyclical. In addition, the yield slope is upward-sloping in expansions and downward-sloping in recessions.First, the countercyclical equity term premium in our model is a result of persistent and countercyclical risk prices. In our model, fundamental shocks simultaneously affect dividend growth and intermediary net worth, which gives rise to the risk premium. The fundamental shocks also affect risk prices. A negative fundamental shock will worsen the banks‚Äô balance sheets, which in turn will increase the price of risk. Since shocks to risk prices are persistent, they have a more significant effect on the price of long-maturity dividend strips. This results in a positive equity term premium. As we discussed earlier, the financial accelerator effect generates a countercyclical risk price; therefore, the equity term premium is also countercyclical.Second, the key element that generates a procyclical equity yield slope is the strong mean reversion of the risk price. From Eq. (8), the term structure slope of the equity yield can be decomposed into an equity term premium component and an expected change in yields component.4 The first component is countercyclical; to have a procyclical yield slope, the expected changes must be procyclical and strong enough. Measuring the tightness of intermedia: In previous sections, we emphasized that our intermediary model‚Äôs ability to explain equity yield slope dynamics relies significantly on the nonlinear feature of risk price within the binding region. This raises a question: in the data, to what extent do fluctuations in the equity yield slope stem from the tightness of intermediary constraints? To address this, we use a model-guided approach to construct a relative tightness index (RT index), which empirically measures the marginal value of intermediary net worth. This index allows us to explore the connection between the tightness of intermediary constraints and asset prices in the data. Additionally, it informs and guides our calibration for the model‚Äôs quantitative analysis.4.1. Portfolios sorted by RT index exposuresIn this subsection, we provide evidence on the relationship between exposure to the RT index and the cross-section of stock returns. We first show that RT exposure negatively predicts the cross-sectional expected stock returns, consistent with the model. Then we conduct an asset pricing test on RT exposure sorted portfolios. Our findings indicate that innovation to the RT index is a negatively priced source of aggregate risk, which lends strong support to our model. Furthermore, a two-factor model with market excess returns and RT index shocks can explain returns of the RT exposure sorted portfolio well.To understand the connection between RT exposure and future stock returns in the cross-section, we construct five portfolios sorted on firms‚Äô RT exposures estimated from a rolling window regression, using 60 months of data prior to the portfolio formation date. Consistent with the model, across the two sets of average returns, firms with low RT exposure earn, on average, subsequently higher returns than firms with high RT exposure. The difference in returns is economically large and statistically significant. The average value-weighted return spread is 6.24% annually, with a t-stat of 2.86. The average equal-weighted return spread is 3.18% annually, with a t-stat of 2.33. From the fact that the RT exposure return spread is smaller in equal-weighted returns than in value-weighted returns, we can infer that the return spread is stronger among large firms.The CAPM cannot explain the pattern of average returns of these portfolios. Quantitative analysis of the model: In this section, we use the RT index to guide our calibration of the intermediary stochastic discount factor and assess the model‚Äôs ability to replicate key moments of equity yields and convenience yields, both unconditionally and conditionally. We present our model calibration and discuss the model parameters in Section 5.1. In Sections 5.2 Aggregate moments, 5.3 Conditional moments, we demonstrate the model‚Äôs performance in reproducing the unconditional and conditional moments of equity yields and convenience yields. Sections 5.4 Equity term structure dynamics, 5.5 Convenience yield dynamics further investigate the model‚Äôs capacity to explain the dynamics of equity yields and convenience yields.5.1. Calibration parametersIn this subsection, we discuss the parameter values in our benchmark calibration, summarized in Table 5. We focus on a long sample of U.S. data (1930‚Äì2020). Internet Appendix F provides more details on our data sources.Following the literature, we set the relative rate of risk aversion, Œ≥, to be 10 and the elasticity of intertemporal substitution, œà, to be 1.5. We set the discount factor, Œ≤, to be 0.9985 to match the level of the risk-free interest rate in the data.For the log output growth process, we calibrate the parameters Œºy and œÉ to broadly match the mean and volatility of consumption growth in the data. Similarly, Œºd is set to match the average log dividend growth rate. We calibrate the additional parameters in the log dividend growth process, œÜ and œÜd, to match the overall volatility of dividend growth and its correlation with consumption.The financial sector parameters include the liquidation probability of banks, Œª; the transfer parameter for new banks, Œ¥; and the fraction of divertible bank assets, Œ∏. As there are no direct empirical counterparts in the data to determine these parameters, we choose them indirectly to broadly match three targets: an average intermediary capital ratio, ENtQt, of 0.21; an average book-to-market ratio, E1Œºt, of 0.65 for the economy-wide financial intermediary sector; and a relative volatility of marginal value growth in recessions, œÉrecŒîlogŒºœÉŒîlogŒº, of 1.67.Following Gertler and Kiyotaki (2010), our model treats the entire intermediary sector as a group of identical institutions. In the model, the capital structure of the intermediary plays a central role in determining asset prices. It is important to match the capital ratio because it affects how consumption shocks get magnified and the probability of being in the constrained versus unconstrained region. We follow the composition of the financial intermediary sector defined in Adrian et al. (2010) to compute the capital ratio of the aggregate financial intermediary from the flow of funds table.9 The average capital ratio over the sample period 1945‚Äì2020 is 0.21. The average value of net worth is another important moment that regulates our calibration of the financial constraint. We compute the book-to-market ratio of the financial industry from CRSP/Compustat. The average book-to-market ratio over the sample period 1950‚Äì2020 is 0.65. The growth rate of a bank‚Äôs marginal net worth value plays a direct role in the augmented stochastic discount factor. Its relative volatility during recessions is instrumental in gauging the non-linearity of asset pricing effects stemming from financial constraints. We base our moment calculation on the estimated RT index. By defining periods where the RT index is in the top 15% as recessions, we measure the volatility of marginal value growth in these recessions relative to its unconditional volatility. For the sample period from 1970 to 2020, this moment value stands at 1.67.From Eq. (30), it is evident that collateralizability parameters, Œ∏FI and Œ∏g, are important in determining the magnitude of convenience yield and government bond premium. We set the collateralizability parameter for government bonds, Œ∏g, at 0.1 to target an average 2-year government bond excess return of 0.6% and the collateralizability parameter for interbank loans, Œ∏FI, at 0.14 to match a convenience yield volatility of 0.18%. These data moments are derived from the sample period spanning 2004 to 2020. We opt to match the convenience yield volatility rather than the convenience yield level, as the convenience yield in the non-binding region is zero in our model, whereas it remains positive in the data. This discrepancy may be attributed to other frictions not accounted for in our model.Table 5. First, both our model and the empirical data depict expansions as periods of high consumption growth and high realized stock returns, whereas recessions are characterized by negative consumption growth and negative stock returns.Second, our model endogenously generates countercyclical volatility of asset returns and marginal value growth. Notably, our model does not feature any time-varying cash flow dynamics. As a result, any fluctuations in volatility arise purely from changes in discount rates. This occurs because, in the low net worth region, the risk price is more sensitive to fundamental shocks, leading to larger asset price changes and higher return volatility. In both cases, the relative volatility surges during recessions, highlighting the highly nonlinear nature of the marginal value of net worth in the binding region and its heightened sensitivity to fundamental shocks during recessions.Third, our model also replicates salient features of equity yield dynamics. However, the magnitude of the slope difference between recessions and expansions is smaller in the model than in the data. This discrepancy arises because our model features i.i.d. normal shocks in consumption growth, and the slope dynamics are solely driven by the discount rate channel. In contrast, the data indicate that the economy exhibits predictable growth rates, leading to more significant cyclical variations in the equity yield slope. In Section 6, we incorporate predictable dividend growth into our baseline model, demonstrating its ability to capture nearly the entire variation in the equity yield slope.To summarize, our model can reasonably reproduce the conditional moments of aggregate quantities and asset prices observed in the data. The model‚Äôs performance in capturing the countercyclical volatility of returns, the conditional volatility of marginal value growth, and the procyclical and sign-switching equity yield slope is noteworthy. Equity term structure dynamicsOur model is also capable of producing a countercyclical equity term premium and procyclical equity yield slope, as well as their close relationship with intermediary state variables, similar to what we observe in the data. Notably, the Œ∏ÀÜ1 estimate from the expectation hypothesis regression is greater than one and closely resembles the data counterpart, indicating that the variation in the equity yield slope in our model, like that in the data, is primarily driven by the expected change component.Our model also performs well when we introduce real intermediary shocks and assess the model-implied equity yield slope in comparison to the actual data. This step is considerably challenging as the shock series does not draw any information from the time-varying equity yield slope. It critically evaluates the model‚Äôs ability to reproduce the equity yield slope dynamics in the face of real-world and potentially non-i.i.d. and non-normal shocks. The success of our model provides strong support for the intermediary mechanism. Convenience yield dynamicsIn this section, we discuss how our model mechanism explains the evidence documented by Van Binsbergen et al. (2021) for the convenience yield, which includes its countercyclical behavior, its predictability for bond excess returns, and its acyclical slope. We also quantitatively confirm that our model can replicate this evidence. In the last subsection, we analyze the behavior of the convenience yield during the COVID-19 crisis and provide insights within the context of our model.5.5.1. Cyclicality of convenience yieldIn our model, the convenience yield and its cyclicality are closely related to the slackness of the participation constraint. In Eq. (30), when the participation constraint is not binding, the loans Rf,t, Rg,t, and RFI,t are equivalent, acting as perfect substitutes. In contrast, we have Rf,t‚â§Rg,t‚â§RFI,t when the intermediary sector is constrained. The parameters Œ∏FI and Œ∏g represent the amount of net worth occupied when investing an additional dollar into these assets. Investing in a security with higher collateralizability occupies less of the net worth. When the economy enters the binding region, the bank‚Äôs net worth becomes increasingly valuable, making investments in assets with low collateralizability more expensive. Market-clearing conditions require the interbank rate and bond yield to increase. The interbank rate will increase more than the bond yield because of the lower collateralizability of interbank loans. This mechanism causes the convenience yield, RFI,t‚àíRg,t, to be countercyclical, spiking during recessions when intermediaries become constrained, which is consistent with the empirical evidence in Van Binsbergen et al. (2021).Quantitatively, the model‚Äôs results regarding the cyclical pattern of convenience yields also align with the data. As highlighted in Table 7, the 12-month model-implied convenience yields spike to 48 basis points during recessions and remain close to 0 in normal times. This mirrors the data‚Äôs counterpart which peaks at 58 basis points during downturns and hovers around 31 basis points during regular times.5.5.2. Bond return predictabilityThe empirical finding that convenience yields can predict the Treasury bond risk premium also aligns with our model prediction. In our model setup, government bonds share the same risk source as convenience yields. Due to the persistence of shocks to bank net worth, long-term government bonds are riskier. Consequently, our model can generate an unconditionally upward-sloping bond yield curve. Furthermore, the term premium of government bonds is countercyclical and shares the same risk source as convenience yields, serving as the primary mechanism for generating bond return predictability. Panel A of Fig. 5 shows the convenience yield and expected 2-year government bond excess return as a function of the net worth share. When the participation constraint is not binding, the convenience yield remains flat at zero. However, as bank net worth falls into the binding region, the convenience yield rises dramatically, along with the expected excess return of 2-year government bonds. Table 9 presents the bond predictability regressions using both the data and our model, confirming the findings of Van Binsbergen et al. (2021) that convenience yields can forecast future government bond returns. Notably, our model successfully reproduces this predictability with a similar magnitude. Convenience yield during COVID-19 crisisDuring the COVID-19 pandemic, the convenience yield exhibited distinctive patterns that have drawn significant attention from the literature. Our model can provide insights to help explain these observations.The upper panels of Fig. 6 display the monthly time series of convenience yields in the U.S. and Euro markets.10 It is evident that convenience yields for both markets spiked during the 2008 financial crisis, and the Euro convenience yield also experienced a spike during the European financial crisis in 2012. The COVID-19 crisis presents an unusual period, as the convenience yield remains relatively flat. The lower panels offer a more granular view of the daily series for the U.S. market during the 2008 financial crisis and the COVID-19 crisis. During the financial crisis, the convenience yield spiked sharply when intermediary constraints tightened. In stark contrast, during the COVID-19 crisis, the convenience yield exhibited an unusual dip, briefly turning ‚Äúinconvenient‚Äù in late March 2020.He et al. (2022) argue that fluctuations in Treasury yields and spreads in March 2020 can be attributed to the interaction between selling pressure from large Treasury holders and intermediation frictions, such as regulatory constraints like the Supplementary Leverage Ratio (SLR), which was implemented after 2008. During this period, investors face higher costs related to reverse Treasury repo funding. They are concerned about potential future liquidity shocks and anticipate a reduced collateral value for Treasuries in future financing.Modeling the external supply of Treasury or regulatory changes is beyond the scope of our paper. However, He et al. (2022)‚Äôs argument can be interpreted as a change in the collateralizability parameter Œ∏g within our model. During the COVID-19 period, because of selling pressure and regulatory change, the collateralizability of Treasury decreases, meaning that investing in Treasuries occupies more bank net worth than before. This corresponds to an increase in Œ∏g. If external factors cause Œ∏g to approach Œ∏FI or even surpass it (Œ∏g>Œ∏FI), a flat or even inverted convenience yield will be observed.

An extended model with predictable di: In this section, we extend our baseline model by introducing predictable dividend growth and evaluate its capability in capturing the dynamics of the equity yield slope.We first dissect the variation of the equity yield slope to understand its source. By decomposing it into two channels ‚Äì the discount rate channel and the expected dividend growth channel ‚Äì we observe that each channel contributes to roughly 50% of the variation. Our baseline intermediary model successfully accounts for the variation stemming from the discount rate channel.Building on this, we incorporate predictable dividend growth into our baseline model. This extended version demonstrates an enhanced ability to account for nearly the entire variation of the equity yield slope.6.1. The discount rate channel and the expected dividend growth channelIn our benchmark model, we purposefully exclude the expected dividend growth channel to focus on investigating the impact of the financial accelerator effect through the discount rate channel. In this section, we first empirically evaluate the relative importance of these two channels. We then evaluate the performance of the intermediary model against other leading asset pricing models, specifically through the discount rate channel.From Eq. (2), it is clear that the forward equity yield depends on both the discount rate and expected dividend growth. Therefore, the equity yield slope also depends on the discount rate slope and expected changes in dividend growth: (38)en,tf‚àíe1,tf=Œ∏n,t‚àíŒ∏1,tÔ∏∏discount rate slope‚àígn,td‚àíg1,tdÔ∏∏expected changes in dividend growth.According to Eq. (38), the forward equity yield will be upward-sloping (downward-sloping) if the discount rate is upward-sloping (downward-sloping) or if the expected average dividend growth rate is decreasing (increasing). Both the procyclical discount rate slope and countercyclical expected changes in dividend growth could contribute to the procyclicality of the equity yield slope.To analyze the properties of discount rate slopes, we use various methods to form dividend growth expectations and subtract them from the equity yield slope. To avoid look-ahead bias, we construct out-of-sample dividend growth expectations using the latent variable model from Jagannathan and Liu (2019), the Bayesian VAR model from Bansal et al. (2021), and IBES data from Ulrich et al. (2023).11 Panel A of Table 11 shows the conditional moments of discount rate slopes derived from these methods. Although displaying less cyclical variation than equity yield slopes, all three methods yield strongly procyclical discount rate slopes, with upward slopes in expansions and downward slopes in recessions.Next, we perform a variance decomposition of the equity yield slope into the discount rate slope and expected changes in dividend growth: (39)varen,tf‚àíe1,tf=coven,tf‚àíe1,tf,Œ∏n,t‚àíŒ∏1,t‚àícov(en,tf‚àíe1,tf,gn,td‚àíg1,td).Dividing both sides of the equation by the variance of the equity yield slope gives a variance decomposition of the equity yield slope into the contribution of the discount rate slope and changes in dividend growth. Panel B of Table 11 presents the results. Across all three methods, the discount rate slope variation accounts for approximately 50% of the equity yield slope fluctuation.To investigate the relationship between the discount rate slope and financial intermediary constraints, we regress the implied discount rate slopes on the RT index. Panel C of Table 11 shows the results. In line with our model predictions, the results indicate a negative relationship between the RT index and discount rate slopes, suggesting that when financial intermediaries face binding constraints, the discount rate is high in the short run, but investors expect it to decrease and mean revert to the steady state in the long run.The cyclical pattern of the equity term structure imposes a strong discipline on the speed of mean reversion for any standard asset pricing model. In Internet Appendix E, we show that in the standard calibration of the long-run risk model (Bansal and Yaron, 2004) and the habit model (Campbell and Cochrane, 1999), the mean reversion speed of discount rates is too slow such that the implied equity yield slope is countercyclical.Our baseline intermediary model generates a procyclical equity yield slope through the strong mean-reversion of discount rates. However, when the expected dividend growth channel is not considered, the model-implied equity yield slope is less volatile than that observed in the data. By extending our model to allow for countercyclical expected changes in dividend growth, we could bring the model-implied equity yield slope dynamics into closer alignment with the data, which we explore in the next subsection.Table 13. A model with predictable growth ratesIn this subsection, we introduce a model that incorporates predictable dividend growth, showcasing its ability in capturing the dynamics of the equity yield slope.In our extended model, we allow the expected growth rate to positively depend on the net worth share. This specification is important for two reasons. article.7. Conclusion: In this paper, we study the cyclical variation of the equity term structure. New empirical facts show that the term structure slope of equity yields is highly procyclical (i.e., positively sloped in expansions and negatively sloped in recessions), whereas the equity term premium is countercyclical. By offering an equity yield decomposition framework, we decompose the term structure of equity yields into an equity term premium and a mean reversion component that reflects expected changes in equity yields. We show that in order to reconcile the seemingly contradictory relationship between equity yield curves and the equity term premium, the mean reversion component must be strong enough to drive the procyclical fluctuations in the overall equity yield term structure.To explain the above-mentioned cyclical patterns, we propose a financial intermediary-based asset pricing model. This model not only captures the cyclical behavior of the equity term structure but also accommodates countercyclical convenience yields that predict bond risk premium, as documented in Van Binsbergen et al. (2021). Within our framework, time-varying leverage constraints on intermediaries endogenously give rise to strong mean reversion in yields. Guided by this model, we develop a novel empirical approach to estimate the relative tightness of these constraints (i.e., the Relative Tightness Index) from the cross-sectional returns of various asset classes. Our empirical and quantitative results confirm that this index significantly influences the slopes of equity yields and the behavior of convenience yields.
